content.text: is everything external to the agent: the systems the agent reads from, acts upon, and must adapt to. The agent itself is the reasoning loop; the environment is everything else — databases, APIs, file systems, web pages, other agents, and the user.
The agent's relationship with its environment is mediated by

(what the agent can do). In LLM agent systems, observations arrive as text in the context window (user messages, tool results, retrieved documents), and actions are tool calls that the host application dispatches.
Environments differ along several dimensions that determine how complex the agent must be:
Partial: agent must maintain internal state to track what it cannot see
Stochastic: same action can yield different results; agent must handle variance
Sequential: past actions affect future states; agent must plan across steps
Dynamic: environment changes while agent deliberates; stale observations mislead
Continuous: infinite action space; agent must constrain via schema and validation
Multi-agent: other agents also act; their actions are part of the environment
Most real LLM agent environments are
partially observable, stochastic, sequential, and dynamic

. The agent sees only what its tools return; tool results can vary; past actions change the state of external systems; and the world keeps changing while the agent deliberates. This combination demands careful state tracking and defensive reasoning.
The Context Window as the Agent's Window onto the Environment
An LLM agent perceives the environment entirely through its

. Whatever is not in the context window does not exist from the model's perspective. This means the agent's view of the environment is inherently partial: it sees only what has been explicitly loaded into the context.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# The agent's environment state: loaded into context on each turn
def build_environment_snapshot(
    current_page_url: str,
    db_record: dict,
    recent_tool_results: list[dict],
) -> str:
    """
    Construct a text snapshot of the current environment state
    to inject into the agent's context.
    """
    lines = [
        "=== Environment Snapshot ===",
        f"Current URL: {current_page_url}",
        f"Database record: {json.dumps(db_record)}",
        "Recent observations:",
    ]
    for obs in recent_tool_results[-3:]:   # last 3 tool results only
        lines.append(f"  [{obs['tool']}] {obs['summary']}")
    return "\n".join(lines)

snapshot = build_environment_snapshot(
    current_page_url="https://shop.example.com/cart",
    db_record={"user_id": 42, "cart_items": 3, "total": 149.99},
    recent_tool_results=[
        {"tool": "search_products", "summary": "Found 12 matching items"},
        {"tool": "add_to_cart",     "summary": "Added Widget Pro (qty 2)"},
    ],
)

resp = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=512,
    system="You are a shopping assistant. Use the environment snapshot to answer user questions.",
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": f"{snapshot}\n\nWhat is in my cart?"},
    ],
)
print(resp.content[0].text)

above explicitly encodes current environment state as a structured text block in the context. This is more reliable than hoping the agent remembers earlier turns: the context may have been windowed, compressed, or started fresh. The snapshot is rebuilt from the real system state each turn.
(what is true in the external world) from
(what the agent believes is true) is critical. They diverge whenever the environment changes without the agent observing it.
"Order #1042 was pending" (from an older tool call)
User said they prefer express shipping
When the agent's belief diverges from the environment state, the agent makes wrong decisions. The fix is
: re-query the environment rather than relying on observations from earlier turns. If an order's status was checked two turns ago, check it again before acting on it.

Dynamic environments change while the agent is working. A user edits a document mid-session; stock prices move between tool calls; another agent concurrently modifies the same database record. Dynamic environments require explicit strategies to avoid acting on stale state.
: read the state, act, then verify the state has not changed before committing. If it changed, re-read and re-decide.
: tag every observation with the time it was fetched. Reject observations older than a threshold before acting.
: minimise the time between observation and action. The longer the reasoning loop, the more the environment can drift. Prefer many short tool calls over one long planning phase.
: always re-fetch the current state immediately before a write tool call, even if the same data was fetched earlier in the same session.

A clean separation between the agent's reasoning and the environment it operates in makes agents easier to test, debug, and swap out. The environment object encapsulates all external state, provides a uniform observation interface, and executes actions.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
import json
import time

@dataclass
class Observation:
    source: str          # which tool or system produced this
    data: dict           # the raw observation
    fetched_at: float    # unix timestamp
    is_fresh: bool = True

    def age_seconds(self) -> float:
        return time.time() - self.fetched_at

    def is_stale(self, max_age: float = 30.0) -> bool:
        return self.age_seconds() > max_age

class AgentEnvironment:
    """
    Wraps external systems the agent can observe and act on.
    All agent perceptions go through this class.
    """

    def __init__(self):
        self._cache: dict[str, Observation] = {}

    def observe(self, key: str, fetch_fn, max_cache_age: float = 30.0) -> Observation:
        """
        Return a fresh or cached observation.
        Re-fetches automatically when the cache is stale.
        """
        cached = self._cache.get(key)
        if cached and not cached.is_stale(max_cache_age):
            return cached
        data = fetch_fn()
        obs = Observation(source=key, data=data, fetched_at=time.time())
        self._cache[key] = obs
        return obs

    def invalidate(self, key: str):
        """Call after any write action that changes this part of the environment."""
        self._cache.pop(key, None)

    def to_context_block(self, keys: list[str]) -> str:
        """Render selected observations as a context injection block."""
        lines = ["=== Environment ==="]
        for key in keys:
            obs = self._cache.get(key)
            if obs:
                age = f"{obs.age_seconds():.0f}s ago"
                lines.append(f"[{key}] (observed {age}): {json.dumps(obs.data)}")
            else:
                lines.append(f"[{key}]: not yet observed")
        return "\n".join(lines)

# Usage
env = AgentEnvironment()

# Observe the order status (fetches from DB stub)
obs = env.observe("order_1042", lambda: {"status": "pending", "items": 2})
print(f"Status: {obs.data['status']}, age: {obs.age_seconds():.2f}s")

# After an update action, invalidate to force re-fetch
env.invalidate("order_1042")

# Context block for injection into prompt
env.observe("order_1042", lambda: {"status": "shipped", "items": 2})
print(env.to_context_block(["order_1042"]))
: every observation records when it was fetched, enabling staleness checks.
seconds are automatically re-fetched on the next access.
to force re-fetch on the next read — preventing the agent from acting on state it just changed.
serialises selected observations into a structured text block for prompt injection.

— the agent can only see a subset of the full state at any moment. A customer service agent cannot see all orders at once; a coding agent cannot read every file; a research agent cannot read every web page simultaneously. Partial observability means the agent must decide
: the agent explicitly explores the environment before planning. It calls a set of read tools to build a broad observation before committing to an action.
: the agent acts on its current (incomplete) view and re-observes only when it encounters a gap in its knowledge. Faster but more prone to errors from missing context.

When multiple agents share an environment, each agent's actions become part of the environment for the others. Agent A's database write can invalidate Agent B's cached observation; two agents assigned the same task may duplicate work or conflict on write operations.
Two agents pick the same task from a queue
Atomic task assignment (DB row lock or Redis SETNX)
Two agents update the same record concurrently
Agent A reads state Agent B is about to change
Short observation TTLs; re-read before write
Agent A waits for B's result; B waits for A's result
Timeout all inter-agent waits; detect cycles
The simplest coordination pattern is a

with atomic claim semantics: each agent atomically marks a task as 'in progress' before working on it, preventing any other agent from claiming the same task. If the agent fails, a timeout releases the task back to the queue.
The environment is the ground truth; the context window is only a snapshot of it.

Agents that treat old observations as current facts make decisions based on a reality that no longer exists. Build agents that re-observe frequently, distinguish their beliefs from the actual environment state, and treat every external system as capable of changing without notice.
